sources.citation: Vilalta, C. J. (2016). Fear of crime in public transport: Research in Mexico City. Crime Prevention and Community Safety, 18(3), 169–183.
sources.relevance: Examines subjective perceptions of security in Mexico, including how citizens evaluate changes in safety over time and across personal versus national levels.
sources.key_finding: Perceptions of insecurity are shaped more by media exposure and neighborhood context than by direct victimization, and personal vs. national assessments often diverge significantly.

sources.citation: Bergman, M., & Flom, H. (2012). Determinants of citizen insecurity perception in Latin America. Latin American Research Review, 47(2), 2–28.
sources.relevance: Directly addresses the measurement of retrospective and prospective security perceptions at personal and national levels across Latin American countries including Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Macro-level crime rates weakly predict individual insecurity perceptions; instead, trust in institutions and media consumption are stronger predictors of perceived security trajectories.

sources.citation: Romero, V., Magaloni, B., & Díaz-Cayeros, A. (2016). Presidential approval and public security in Mexico's war on crime. Latin American Politics and Society, 58(2), 100–123.
sources.relevance: Analyzes how citizens' evaluations of national security trends (retrospective and prospective) influence political attitudes, linking security trajectory perceptions to political outcomes.
sources.key_finding: Negative retrospective evaluations of national security significantly reduce presidential approval, while prospective optimism partially buffers this effect.

sources.citation: Hale, C. (1996). Fear of crime: A review of the literature. International Review of Victimology, 4(2), 79–150.
sources.relevance: Provides foundational discussion of the distinction between personal and societal risk assessments in fear of crime research, relevant to the two-level aggregation in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Individuals consistently rate national crime problems as worse than personal risk, a phenomenon known as the 'personal-national gap' in security perceptions.

sources.citation: Tyler, T. R. (2006). Why people obey the law. Princeton University Press.
sources.relevance: Foundational work on procedural justice and legitimacy theory, directly relevant to the normative compliance items (p52, p53) measuring whether police authority should be obeyed.
sources.key_finding: People comply with legal authorities primarily because they perceive them as legitimate and fair, not because of fear of punishment.

sources.citation: Sabet, D. M. (2012). Police reform in Mexico: Informal politics and the challenge of institutional change. Stanford University Press.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the specific challenges of police legitimacy in Mexico, including corruption, human rights abuses, and the fragmented municipal police system referenced in the survey items.
sources.key_finding: Mexican police reform efforts are undermined by deeply entrenched informal practices, and public perceptions of corruption are a primary driver of low police legitimacy.

sources.citation: Tankebe, J. (2013). Viewing things differently: The dimensions of public perceptions of police legitimacy. Criminology, 51(1), 103–135.
sources.relevance: Proposes a multidimensional model of police legitimacy encompassing effectiveness, procedural fairness, distributive fairness, and lawfulness—dimensions captured by the performance, corruption, and human rights items.
sources.key_finding: Police legitimacy is a multidimensional construct where perceived lawfulness and effectiveness independently contribute to willingness to cooperate with police.

sources.citation: Artz, S. (2012). Militarización, seguridad pública y policía comunitaria en México. In J. Bailey & L. Dammert (Eds.), Seguridad y reforma policial en las Américas. Siglo XXI.
sources.relevance: Discusses the Mexican context of police performance evaluation and citizen satisfaction with policing at the municipal level, directly relevant to the construct's survey items.
sources.key_finding: Municipal police in Mexico suffer from the lowest levels of public trust among security institutions, driven by perceptions of corruption and ineffectiveness.

sources.citation: Sunshine, J., & Tyler, T. R. (2003). The role of procedural justice and legitimacy in shaping public support for policing. Law & Society Review, 37(3), 513–548.
sources.relevance: Empirically tests the relationship between procedural justice perceptions, legitimacy, and compliance with police, directly relevant to the obligation-to-obey items.
sources.key_finding: Procedural justice is the primary driver of police legitimacy, which in turn predicts both compliance with the law and willingness to cooperate with police.

sources.citation: INEGI. (2022). Encuesta Nacional de Victimización y Percepción sobre Seguridad Pública (ENVIPE) 2022: Marco conceptual. Instituto Nacional de Estadística y Geografía.
sources.relevance: Provides the standard Mexican measurement framework for crime victimization including household vs. personal victimization, crime type disaggregation, and reporting behavior—directly mirroring this construct's items.
sources.key_finding: Mexico's dark figure of crime (cifra negra) exceeds 93%, making self-reported victimization surveys essential for understanding true crime exposure.

sources.citation: Vilalta, C. J., & Muggah, R. (2016). What explains criminal violence in Mexico City? A test of two theories of crime. Stability: International Journal of Security and Development, 5(1), 1–22.
sources.relevance: Uses victimization survey data in Mexico to test ecological and opportunity theories of crime, demonstrating how victimization frequency and type vary across urban contexts.
sources.key_finding: Routine activity patterns and socioeconomic inequality are stronger predictors of victimization than demographic characteristics alone in Mexican urban settings.

sources.citation: Skogan, W. G. (1987). The impact of victimization on fear. Crime & Delinquency, 33(1), 135–154.
sources.relevance: Establishes the causal pathway from victimization to fear of crime, relevant to understanding how this construct relates to fear_and_risk_avoidance.
sources.key_finding: Direct victimization increases fear of crime, but the effect is moderated by crime type, with violent victimization producing stronger and more lasting fear responses.

sources.citation: Soares, R. R. (2004). Development, crime and punishment: Accounting for the international differences in crime rates. Journal of Development Economics, 73(1), 155–184.
sources.relevance: Provides a cross-national framework linking crime victimization rates to development indicators, relevant to understanding Mexican victimization in comparative context.
sources.key_finding: Underreporting of crime is systematically higher in developing countries, and victimization rates are strongly associated with inequality and weak institutional capacity.

sources.citation: Ferraro, K. F. (1995). Fear of crime: Interpreting victimization risk. SUNY Press.
sources.relevance: Provides the theoretical distinction between cognitive risk assessment (perceived probability) and affective fear responses, both of which are captured in this construct's items (p11 vs. p12/p13).
sources.key_finding: Fear of crime and perceived risk of victimization are related but distinct constructs; conflating them leads to measurement error and inflated prevalence estimates.

sources.citation: Jackson, J. (2009). A psychological perspective on vulnerability in the fear of crime. Psychology, Crime & Law, 15(4), 365–390.
sources.relevance: Explains how perceived vulnerability translates into behavioral adaptations (avoidance, activity restriction), directly relevant to the behavioral items p22_1 and p23_1.
sources.key_finding: Perceived vulnerability mediates the relationship between environmental cues and constrained behavior, with women and older adults showing stronger avoidance responses.

sources.citation: Vilalta, C. J. (2012). Fear of crime in gated communities and apartment buildings: A comparison of housing types and a test of theories. Journal of Housing and the Built Environment, 27(1), 107–120.
sources.relevance: Studies fear of crime and avoidance behaviors specifically in the Mexican urban context, providing empirical validation for measuring these constructs in Mexican populations.
sources.key_finding: Residential context significantly shapes fear of crime in Mexico, with gated community residents reporting lower fear but similar avoidance behaviors compared to apartment dwellers.

sources.citation: Braakmann, N. (2012). How do individuals deal with victimization and victimization risk? Longitudinal evidence from Mexico. Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization, 84(1), 335–344.
sources.relevance: Directly examines behavioral responses to crime risk in Mexico, including activity cessation and avoidance strategies, using panel data.
sources.key_finding: Victimization leads to significant increases in avoidance behaviors and private security expenditures, with effects persisting for at least one year after the victimization event.

sources.citation: Rader, N. E. (2004). The threat of victimization: A theoretical reconceptualization of fear of crime. Sociological Spectrum, 24(6), 689–704.
sources.relevance: Proposes the 'threat of victimization' model integrating fear (emotion), perceived risk (cognition), and constrained behaviors (action)—the three components captured in this construct.
sources.key_finding: Fear, perceived risk, and constrained behavior form a unified threat-of-victimization construct that better predicts quality of life outcomes than any single component alone.

sources.citation: Zizumbo-Colunga, D. (2017). Community, authorities, and support for vigilantism: Experimental evidence. Political Behavior, 39(4), 989–1015.
sources.relevance: Directly examines support for vigilante justice in Mexico, testing how institutional failure and community identity drive endorsement of extralegal responses—the core of items p66 and p68.
sources.key_finding: Support for vigilantism in Mexico increases significantly when citizens perceive that formal authorities have failed, and community identification amplifies this effect.

sources.citation: Malone, M. F. T. (2012). The rule of law in Central America: Citizens' reactions to crime and punishment. Bloomsbury Academic.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the trade-off between security and liberty (p67) and support for mano dura policies in Latin America, including capital punishment attitudes (p71).
sources.key_finding: Citizens who perceive high crime and low institutional effectiveness are more likely to support authoritarian security measures and sacrifice civil liberties for safety.

sources.citation: Bateson, R. (2012). Crime victimization and political participation. American Political Science Review, 106(3), 570–587.
sources.relevance: Examines how crime victimization shapes political attitudes including support for extralegal violence and punitive policies in Latin America.
sources.key_finding: Crime victims are more likely to support vigilante justice and punitive policies, but also more likely to engage in conventional political participation.

sources.citation: Godoy, A. S. (2006). Popular injustice: Violence, community, and law in Latin America. Stanford University Press.
sources.relevance: Provides ethnographic and theoretical analysis of lynching and community justice in Latin America, contextualizing the vigilante attitudes measured by p68.
sources.key_finding: Community self-justice in Latin America reflects not merely lawlessness but a competing normative framework rooted in collective sovereignty claims when state institutions are absent or corrupt.

sources.citation: Magaloni, B., Franco-Vivanco, E., & Melo, V. (2020). Killing in the name of the state: Mexico's drug war and targeted violence. American Political Science Review, 114(3), 726–743.
sources.relevance: Analyzes public attitudes toward state violence in Mexico's drug war, directly relevant to p72 (agreement with federal government actions against drug trafficking) and p66 (violence to combat violence).
sources.key_finding: Public support for militarized anti-drug operations persists even when accompanied by human rights violations, driven by fear and perceptions of criminal threat severity.
